preamble: The diphthong [au] can undergo a phonetic transformation where its second vocalic element, the close back rounded vowel [u], strengthens into a voiced labiodental fricative [v]. This process, known as consonantization and fricativization, results in a consonantal outcome for the latter part of the original diphthong.
phoneticEffects: Consonantization of the vocalic glide, fricativization, diphthong reduction

examples.note: The Ancient Greek diphthong /au̯/ evolved to /av/ in Modern Greek when followed by a voiced consonant or a vowel. For example, Ancient Greek αὐλός (aulós) is pronounced /avˈlos/ in Modern Greek.

sources: Sihler, A. L. (1995). New Comparative Grammar of Greek and Latin. Oxford University Press.
